title: Human Anatomy & Histology
subtitle: Gross anatomy, tissue structures, musculoskeletal geography & histology
methodology: METHODOLOGY · Every entry anchors to a landmark study, paper or theoretical text. The hint explains the idea in plain language; the note names the primary publication and flags replication status, major revisions and ongoing debates. Studies and effects carry a replicability badge — 🟢 robust · 🟡 nuanced or reduced · 🔴 failed or severely contested. Map pins mark the institution where the work was carried out.

categories.label: Theories & Models
categories.subtitle: Conceptual frameworks that organised anatomical and histological knowledge

entries.label: Cell Theory
entries.hint: All living organisms consist of one or more cells; the cell is the basic structural and functional unit of life; and all cells arise from pre-existing cells. Schleiden (1838) applied this to plants, Schwann (1839) to animals, and Virchow (1855) supplied the third tenet — 'omnis cellula e cellula' — completing the framework that redirected biology from organs to cells as the primary level of organisation.
entries.note: Schleiden, M. J. (1838). 'Beiträge zur Phytogenesis.' Archiv für Anatomie. Schwann, T. (1839). Microscopical Researches into the Accordance in the Structure and Growth of Animals and Plants. Sydenham Society. Virchow, R. (1855). 'Cellular-Pathologie.' Archiv für pathologische Anatomie, 8, 3–39. The theory resolved the debate between humoral and solidist medicine in favour of cells as the locus of pathology.

entries.label: Germ Layer Theory
entries.hint: The vertebrate embryo is organised from three primary tissue layers: ectoderm (outer), mesoderm (middle), and endoderm (inner). Each layer gives rise to specific organs and tissues with near-perfect fidelity: ectoderm → epidermis and nervous system; mesoderm → muscle, bone, cardiovascular and urogenital systems; endoderm → gut epithelium, liver, lungs and pancreas. The theory predicts organ derivation and explains why teratomas contain tissue from multiple layers.
entries.note: Pander, H. C. (1817). Beiträge zur Entwickelungsgeschichte des Hühnchens im Eye — first description of three layers in chick embryo. von Baer, K. E. (1828). Über Entwickelungsgeschichte der Thiere — generalised to all vertebrates and named the layers. Remak, R. (1855) named and defined the three layers definitively. The germ layer framework is the conceptual anchor of both embryology and developmental biology.

entries.label: Wolff's Law of Bone Remodelling
entries.hint: Bone adapts its internal architecture and external form to the magnitude and direction of the habitual mechanical forces it experiences. Regions under compression develop denser trabeculae; unstressed regions lose bone. Wolff derived the mathematical prediction — that trabecular alignment mirrors principal stress trajectories — from Culmann's graphical statics of a crane and Fairbairn's tests on wrought iron.
entries.note: Wolff, J. (1892). Das Gesetz der Transformation der Knochen. August Hirschwald, Berlin. Piezoelectric basis: Fukada, E. & Yasuda, I. (1957) Journal of the Physical Society of Japan — mechanical stress generates electrical potentials directing osteoblast activity. The law explains osteoporotic trabecular thinning, athlete bone density, and distraction osteogenesis (Ilizarov). Precursors: Roux (1881); explicit engineering analogy by Culmann.

entries.label: Harvey's Closed-Circuit Circulation
entries.hint: Blood does not ebb and flow from the liver but circulates continuously in a closed loop driven by the heart as a mechanical pump. The conceptual shift: the same blood recirculates — it is not continuously manufactured and consumed — and the circuit is anatomically closed, with venous valves enforcing directional flow from periphery back to heart. Capillaries completing the arteriovenous connection were confirmed by Malpighi (1661) four years after Harvey's death.
entries.note: Harvey, W. (1628). Exercitatio Anatomica de Motu Cordis et Sanguinis in Animalibus. Fitzeri, Frankfurt. Capillaries confirming the circuit were discovered by Malpighi (1661). Refuted Galen's model of blood produced in liver, consumed by tissues, and a pneuma-enriched arterial stream. Opposition from Galenic physicians persisted into the 1650s but was overcome as Harvey's ligature experiments were reproduced across Europe.

entries.label: Tensegrity Model of Cell Mechanics
entries.hint: The cell maintains its shape through tensional integrity: actin microfilaments carry continuous tensile prestress while microtubules and crosslinked ECM bear discrete compression. External mechanical forces are transmitted through this lattice directly to the nucleus and chromatin, altering gene expression — the cellular basis of mechanotransduction. Disrupting tension collapses cell form; adding external compression stiffens it, exactly as a geodesic dome behaves.
entries.note: Ingber, D. E. (1993). 'Cellular tensegrity: defining new rules of biological design that govern the cytoskeleton.' Journal of Cell Science, 104, 613–627. Mechanotransduction: Wang, N. et al. (1993) Science — integrins as mechanosensors. Clinical relevance: cancer cells have altered cytoskeletal tension; tumour stiffness (AFM) predicts invasiveness. The tensegrity model unified ECM, cytoskeleton, and nuclear mechanics into a single force-transmission hierarchy.

entries.label: Dermatome Map & Segmental Innervation
entries.hint: Each dorsal root ganglion and its spinal segment innervates a predictable band of skin (dermatome) and a group of muscles (myotome). The map predicts sensory loss after spinal nerve injury and the sites of referred pain from visceral disease: cardiac ischemia refers to C8–T1 territory (left arm, jaw); appendicitis initially refers to T10 (periumbilical). Adjacent dermatomal overlap means single-root lesions produce incomplete, not total, sensory loss.
entries.note: Head, H. (1893). 'On disturbances of sensation with especial reference to the pain of visceral disease.' Brain, 16, 1–133 — first systematic dermatomal map derived from herpes zoster lesion boundaries. Foerster, O. (1933) Brain — definitive map from posterior root section in humans. Modern maps still disagree by up to 2 segments: Lee, M. W. et al. (2008); Downs, M. B. (2011) Spine. Clinical pearl: L4 → medial shin; L5 → dorsum foot; S1 → lateral foot and sole.

entries.label: Anatomical Nomenclature & Terminologia Anatomica
entries.hint: The 1895 Basle Nomina Anatomica (BNA) replaced ~30,000 eponymous and vernacular terms with a single Latin vocabulary — the first international consensus in medicine. Anatomical position (standing, arms supinated), directional terms (anterior/posterior, medial/lateral, proximal/distal, superior/inferior), and body plane definitions (sagittal, coronal, transverse) are its lasting contribution. The current standard, Terminologia Anatomica (1998), lists ~7,500 terms.
entries.note: His, W. (1895) — BNA published by the German Anatomical Society in Basel. Revisions: BNA → JNA (1936) → PNA (1955) → FCAT Terminologia Anatomica (1998). Eponymic terms persist clinically (Fallopian tube, Eustachian tube, Circle of Willis) and cause diagnostic ambiguity; standardisation is ongoing. The BNA reduced four competing nomenclatures to one, enabling international anatomical communication.

entries.label: Fascia as a Bodywide Tensional Network
entries.hint: Myers' Anatomy Trains model (2001) proposes that myofascial continuities transmit tensile force across multiple joints and body segments — superseding the view of muscles as isolated force-generators between fixed origin and insertion. 'Myofascial meridians' (e.g., Superficial Back Line: plantar fascia → Achilles → hamstrings → thoracolumbar fascia → erector spinae → epicranial aponeurosis) transmit tension from heel to scalp. Schleip et al. (2012) provided histological evidence: fascia is innervated, contractile, and proprioceptive.
entries.note: Myers, T. W. (2001). Anatomy Trains: Myofascial Meridians for Manual and Movement Therapists. Churchill Livingstone. Schleip, R. et al. (2012). Fascia: The Tensional Network of the Human Body. Churchill Livingstone. Huijing, P. A. (1999) Journal of Biomechanics — myofascial force transmission demonstrated in rat muscle. Evidence status: cross-body myofascial force transmission in intact humans remains contested. In vitro and cadaveric studies support mechanical continuity; controlled clinical trials demonstrating force transmission across the proposed meridians in living subjects are limited and methodologically heterogeneous (Wilke, J. et al. 2016 JOSPT; van der Wal 2009 Cells Tissues Organs). The model's clinical applications — plantar fasciitis responding to hamstring stretching, distal effects of local fascial release — are widely practised but lack the mechanistic proof the model implies. Classification controversy: see db4.

categories.label: Landmark Studies
categories.subtitle: Experiments, observations and atlases that established the structure of the human body

entries.label: Vesalius — De Humani Corporis Fabrica
entries.hint: Seven books of systematic cadaveric dissection, illustrated by Jan van Calcar (Titian's studio), corrected ~200 Galenic errors made from animal dissection. Vesalius demonstrated that the human sternum has three segments (not seven), the jaw is a single bone, and the interventricular septum has no pores. The Fabrica established anatomy as an empirical observational science practised by the physician-dissector, not delegated to a barber-surgeon reading aloud from Galen.
entries.note: Vesalius, A. (1543). De Humani Corporis Fabrica Libri Septem. Oporinus, Basel. Simultaneously published the Epitome — an abridged student edition. The Fabrica's seven books: bones and ligaments, muscles, vasculature, nervous system, abdominal organs, thoracic organs, brain and cranial nerves. Vesalius's self-portrait dissecting a female forearm symbolically displaced Galen as the authority. The book was reprinted in 1555 with revisions.

entries.label: Harvey — De Motu Cordis
entries.hint: Harvey tied ligatures around the arm at different tightnesses to demonstrate venous valves enforcing one-way flow, then calculated the volume pumped per hour to be far more than the body contains — requiring recirculation. Vivisections on snakes (slow enough to observe cardiac cycles) showed the apex contracts inward during systole. The experiments were reproducible, quantitative, and anatomically falsifiable — a model of experimental anatomy.
entries.note: Harvey, W. (1628). Exercitatio Anatomica de Motu Cordis et Sanguinis in Animalibus. W. Fitzer, Frankfurt. Malpighi confirmed capillaries in frog lung in 1661 (st5), completing the anatomical circuit. Opposition from Galenic physicians (James Primrose, Emilio Parisano) was eventually overcome as ligature experiments were reproduced across Europe. Harvey also wrote an embryology text (1651): 'ex ovo omnia' — all animals arise from eggs.

entries.label: Virchow — Cellular Pathology
entries.hint: 'Omnis cellula e cellula' — every cell arises from a pre-existing cell. Virchow used histological sections to demonstrate that pathological tissue (tumours, inflamed tissue, necrotic areas) consists of abnormal cells derived from normal ones, not from spontaneous generation. This reoriented medicine from humoral to cellular: disease is the life of cells under altered conditions — a framework that directly created the discipline of histopathology.
entries.note: Virchow, R. (1858). Die Cellularpathologie in ihrer Begründung auf physiologische und pathologische Gewebelehre. August Hirschwald, Berlin. Virchow also named leukaemia, thrombosis, embolism, and amyloid degeneration in the same decade. His political engagement (Reichstag, public health reform) made him a model of physician-scientist-citizen. Translation: Cellular Pathology (1860, Frank Chance).

entries.label: Flemming — Discovery of Mitosis
entries.hint: Using aniline dyes to stain salamander fin cells with a high-resolution oil-immersion microscope, Flemming described and named chromatin (the dye-accepting nuclear substance) and mitosis (from Greek mitos, thread). He identified and named prophase, metaphase, and anaphase; described chromosome splitting and the equatorial plate; and demonstrated that new nuclei arise exclusively from pre-existing nuclei — completing Virchow's rule at the subcellular level.
entries.note: Flemming, W. (1882). Zellsubstanz, Kern und Zelltheilung. F. C. W. Vogel, Leipzig. Flemming did not discover chromosomes (described by Schneider in 1873) but provided the definitive cytological account of their behaviour during division. Meiosis was described by van Beneden (1883) in Ascaris. The term 'mitosis' and its visual framework — threads separating to poles — enabled all subsequent chromosome theory and Mendel reintegration.

entries.label: Malpighi — Discovery of Capillaries
entries.hint: Using a rudimentary compound microscope with 30–50× magnification, Malpighi observed blood passing through a fine network of vessels in the dried and inflated lung of a frog — the capillaries Harvey had predicted but could not see. This closed Harvey's circulatory loop anatomically and confirmed that arteries and veins are connected by a third vessel class. Malpighi also demonstrated capillary exchange in the bladder, intestine, and skin.
entries.note: Malpighi, M. (1661). De Pulmonibus Observationes Anatomicae. Letters to Borelli. Bologna. Malpighi's subsequent histological discoveries include the renal glomerulus (Malpighian corpuscle), splenic white pulp (Malpighian body), taste buds, and deep epidermal layers (stratum Malpighii / stratum spinosum). He established microscopic anatomy as a discipline and was elected Fellow of the Royal Society.

entries.label: Röntgen — First Diagnostic X-ray
entries.hint: On 8 November 1895, Röntgen noticed a barium platinocyanide screen glowing across the room when a cathode-ray tube was energised — even though the tube was shielded. The X-ray penetrated matter in proportion to density: soft tissue was translucent; bone, opaque. The first medical image was an X-ray of his wife Anna's hand showing bones and her wedding ring. Within six weeks X-rays were being used clinically to locate bullets and fractures.
entries.note: Röntgen, W. C. (1895). 'Über eine neue Art von Strahlen.' Sitzungsberichte der Physikalisch-medizinischen Gesellschaft zu Würzburg, 9, 132–141. Nobel Prize in Physics 1901 — the first Nobel Prize awarded. Clinical adoption was immediate: X-ray units deployed in the Balkan War by 1897. Long-term radiation risks were not appreciated for decades; many early radiologists, including Marie Curie, died of aplastic anaemia or radiation-induced cancers.

entries.label: Cajal — Histología del Sistema Nervioso
entries.hint: Using Golgi's silver chromate stain (which paradoxically stains only 1–5% of neurons, making the rest transparent), Cajal demonstrated that neurons are individual discrete cells — not a continuous reticulum as Golgi believed. He described axon growth cones in chick embryos, dendritic spines, the direction of impulse flow (axon out, dendrites in), and mapped visual and cerebellar cortex pathways with extraordinary precision that electron microscopy later confirmed.
entries.note: Cajal, S. R. y (1899–1904). Histología del Sistema Nervioso del Hombre y de los Vertebrados (2 vols). Moya, Madrid. Nobel Prize 1906, shared with Golgi — despite their fundamental disagreement about the neuron doctrine (Golgi maintained the reticular theory in his own Nobel lecture). Cajal's drawings remain anatomically accurate; many were not bettered by electron microscopy until the 1950s. He is considered the father of modern neuroscience.

entries.label: McCulloch & Till — Bone Marrow Stem Cells
entries.loc: Ontario Cancer Institute, Toronto, Ontario, Canada
entries.hint: Lethally irradiated mice given intravenous bone marrow cells from healthy donors developed discrete spleen nodules after 10–14 days — each colony derived from a single transplanted progenitor. By varying dose and re-transplanting colonies, McCulloch and Till demonstrated a self-renewing, multipotent progenitor capable of producing all blood cell lineages — the first experimental demonstration of a stem cell. The colony-forming unit assay they developed remains in use.
entries.note: Till, J. E. & McCulloch, E. A. (1961). 'A direct measurement of the radiation sensitivity of normal mouse bone marrow cells.' Radiation Research, 14, 213–222. Becker, A. J. et al. (1963) Nature — proved colonies are clonal. Haematopoietic stem cell isolation: Weissman, I. L. et al. (1988) PNAS — Lin-Sca-1+ cells. Foundation of bone marrow transplantation, CAR-T therapy, and all subsequent stem cell biology.

entries.label: Visible Human Project
entries.loc: National Library of Medicine, Bethesda, Maryland
entries.hint: A male cadaver and female cadaver were each CT and MRI-scanned before being embedded in gelatin, frozen, and serially sectioned. Male (1994): 1,871 transverse cryosections at 1mm; female (1995): 5,189 sections at 0.33mm. Each section was colour-photographed. The freely available dataset established the standard for digital anatomical atlases, surgical simulation software, and 3D anatomical reconstruction — making gross anatomy accessible beyond the dissection room.
entries.note: Ackerman, M. J. (1998). 'The Visible Human Project.' Proceedings of the IEEE, 86, 504–511. The male cadaver was Joseph Paul Jernigan, an executed prisoner (1993). Dataset available at nlm.nih.gov/research/visible. Used as basis for Anatomage, BioDigital Human, and multiple surgical planning platforms. The project raised ethical debate about cadaveric consent and use of executed prisoners' remains.

entries.label: Gray & Carter — Gray's Anatomy
entries.hint: Henry Gray wrote the text; Henry Vandyke Carter produced 363 original woodcut illustrations using a camera lucida. The organisation — by region and system — and the clarity of Carter's drawings set the template for anatomical education. Two years of dissection at St George's produced the most influential anatomical textbook in history, now in its 42nd edition (2020). Gray's systemic-regional structure has never been superseded.
entries.note: Gray, H. (1858). Anatomy: Descriptive and Surgical. John W. Parker and Son, London. Carter received no royalties and no co-authorship on the title page — acknowledged only in the introduction. An American edition appeared in 1859. Gray died of confluent smallpox in 1861, aged ~34. The 41st edition (Churchill Livingstone, 2016): 1,562 pages, 2,100 illustrations. The book remains in continuous print — one of the longest-lived medical texts.

entries.label: Uhlén et al. — Human Protein Atlas
entries.loc: KTH Royal Institute of Technology, Stockholm, Sweden
entries.hint: Systematic immunohistochemical staining of tissue microarrays with antibodies against every human protein, providing a spatial expression map of the human proteome at single-cell resolution across 44 normal tissue types. Annotated images at proteinatlas.org. The atlas enabled identification of tissue-specific proteins, novel cancer biomarkers, and tissue-selective drug targets — turning histology from a morphological to a molecular discipline.
entries.note: Uhlén, M. et al. (2005). 'A human protein atlas for normal and cancer tissues based on antibody proteomics.' Molecular & Cellular Proteomics, 4, 1920–1932. As of 2023: antibodies for >24,000 proteins; subcellular localisation atlas; single-cell RNA atlas. Nature Methods 2022 Method of the Year. The atlas revealed that ~70% of human proteins are expressed in the testis — the most transcriptionally active human tissue.

categories.label: Effects & Phenomena
categories.subtitle: Named anatomical and histological phenomena with diagnostic or functional significance

entries.label: Bone Adaptive Remodelling (Wolff's Law In Vivo)
entries.hint: Removal of one ulna in an animal causes the radius to hypertrophy and remodel into a load-bearing configuration indistinguishable from a normal ulna. Immobilisation (casting) causes trabecular thinning measurable by radiograph within 6 weeks. Tennis players have 20–30% greater cortical thickness in the dominant arm. Astronauts lose 0.5–1% bone mineral density per month in microgravity — and recover it within months of return. Ilizarov's distraction osteogenesis grows new bone by applying controlled tension across an osteotomy gap.
entries.note: Wolff, J. (1892) quantified the observational record. X-ray quantification: Meunier, P. J. et al. (1973) — trabecular density changes in immobilisation. Spaceflight: Leblanc, A. et al. (2000) Journal of Bone and Mineral Research — 1–2% BMD loss/month in weight-bearing bones. Distraction osteogenesis: Ilizarov, G. A. (1989) Clinical Orthopaedics — the Ilizarov fixator applies 1mm/day tension, generating bone from callus.

entries.label: Contact Inhibition of Proliferation
entries.hint: Normal cells in culture cease dividing when they contact neighbouring cells and form a monolayer — they 'sense' local density and halt the cell cycle. Cancer cells lose this constraint and pile up into disordered multilayers. The mechanism involves the cadherin–Hippo signalling pathway (YAP/TAZ nuclear exclusion upon crowding) and contact-activated receptor inactivation. Loss of contact inhibition is one of Hanahan & Weinberg's Hallmarks of Cancer (2000, 2011).
entries.note: Abercrombie, M. & Heaysman, J. E. M. (1954). 'Observations on the social behaviour of cells in tissue culture.' Experimental Cell Research, 6, 293–306. Molecular mechanism: Zhao, B. et al. (2007) Genes & Development — Hippo pathway connects cell contact to YAP/TAZ nuclear exclusion. Hanahan, D. & Weinberg, R. A. (2000) Cell; updated (2011) Cell — six (then ten) hallmarks of cancer, including evasion of contact inhibition. Loss of contact inhibition is a defining property of all malignant cell lines.

entries.label: Apoptosis — Programmed Cell Death
entries.hint: Kerr, Wyllie and Currie named and described a morphologically distinct form of cell death: nuclear condensation, chromatin margination, cytoplasmic shrinkage, membrane blebbing, and fragmentation into apoptotic bodies that are phagocytosed without inflammation — in contrast to necrosis, which causes lysis and inflammatory response. Apoptosis is genetically controlled: caspase proteases execute the programme; Bcl-2 family proteins regulate it. Essential for digit separation, neural pruning, and immune selection.
entries.note: Kerr, J. F. R., Wyllie, A. H. & Currie, A. R. (1972). 'Apoptosis: a basic biological phenomenon with wide-ranging implications in tissue kinetics.' British Journal of Cancer, 26, 239–257. Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2002 (Brenner, Horvitz, Sulston) for the genetic programme of apoptosis in C. elegans. Bcl-2: Vaux, D. L. et al. (1988) Nature — first anti-apoptotic protein from follicular lymphoma translocation t(14;18). Caspase cascade: Salvesen & Dixit (1997) Science.

entries.label: Referred Pain & Dermatomal Pattern
entries.loc: National Hospital for Paralysis, London, England
entries.hint: Pain originating in a visceral organ is perceived in the somatic skin or muscle territory sharing the same spinal segment — because both converge on a common second-order neuron in the dorsal horn. Classic examples: myocardial ischemia → left arm and jaw (C8–T1); appendicitis → periumbilical then right iliac fossa (T10, then T12–L1); diaphragm irritation → shoulder tip (C3–C5 phrenic nerve). Referred pain localises the organ's embryological segmental origin.
entries.note: Head, H. (1893) Brain — first systematic mapping of referred pain from herpes zoster and visceral disease. Convergence-projection theory: Ruch, T. C. (1946) — visceral and somatic afferents converge on the same dorsal horn neuron, causing the brain to misattribute visceral pain to the somatic territory. Clinical application: referred shoulder pain after laparoscopy (diaphragmatic irritation from CO₂); Kehr's sign (left shoulder pain from splenic haematoma) — C3–C5.

entries.label: Disuse Muscle Atrophy
entries.hint: Skeletal muscle loses 0.5–1% of cross-sectional area per day of complete unloading. After 2 weeks of bed rest, quadriceps CSA falls ~10%; after 6 weeks of casting, up to 30%. The mechanism: FoxO transcription factors activate atrogin-1 and MuRF-1 E3 ubiquitin ligases that target myosin heavy chain for proteasomal degradation. Spaceflight causes equivalent loss in postural muscles (soleus, vastus lateralis) within days. Resistance exercise prevents and reverses atrophy through IGF-1/PI3K/Akt/mTOR signalling.
entries.note: Henriksson, J. & Reitman, J. S. (1976) Acta Physiologica Scandinavica — early quantitative data on disuse atrophy. UPS pathway: Bodine, S. C. et al. (2001) Science — atrogin-1/MAFbx as atrophy-specific ubiquitin ligase. Spaceflight: Narici, M. V. & Maganaris, C. N. (2007) Physiological Reviews — comprehensive review of unloading effects. Clinical relevance: intensive care unit patients lose ~2% muscle mass/day; early physiotherapy is prescribed to combat this.

entries.label: Regenerative Capacity Gradient
entries.hint: Tissues differ profoundly in regenerative potential. Intestinal epithelium renews completely every 4–5 days; liver regenerates 75% mass after partial hepatectomy within weeks; skeletal muscle regenerates from satellite cells but with some scar; cardiac muscle has negligible regenerative capacity after infarction; CNS neurons do not regenerate axons (myelin-associated inhibitory factors: Nogo-A, MAG, OMgp). The gradient correlates with mitotic activity and predicts clinical strategy: cardiac damage is permanent; hepatotoxicity is reversible; intestinal irradiation is rapidly fatal.
entries.note: Liver regeneration: Fausto, N. (2004) Hepatology — mechanisms of compensatory hypertrophy after hepatectomy. CNS regeneration failure: Schwab, M. E. & Bartholdi, D. (1996) — Nogo-A and myelin-associated inhibitors. Satellite cells: Mauro, A. (1961) Journal of Biophysical and Biochemical Cytology — discovery of satellite cells in skeletal muscle. Heart C14 dating: Bergmann, O. et al. (2009) Science — ~1% cardiomyocyte renewal/year, declining with age.

entries.label: Haematopoietic Hierarchy & Lineage Commitment
entries.loc: Ontario Cancer Institute, Toronto, Ontario, Canada
entries.hint: All blood cell types — erythrocytes, platelets, granulocytes, monocytes, B and T lymphocytes, NK cells — derive from a single multipotent haematopoietic stem cell (HSC) through progressive lineage restriction. Common myeloid progenitors give rise to erythrocytes and myeloid cells; common lymphoid progenitors to lymphocytes. Each commitment step is driven by a transcription factor cascade (GATA-1 for erythroid; C/EBPα for myeloid; Ikaros for lymphoid). The hierarchy explains leukaemia subtypes and bone marrow transplantation.
entries.note: McCulloch & Till (1961) demonstrated multipotency; Weissman, I. L. et al. (1988) PNAS — HSC isolation (Lin-Sca-1+). Transcription factor control: Orkin, S. H. & Zon, L. I. (2008) Cell — seminal review of haematopoietic transcription factors. The hierarchy is used clinically to classify leukaemia (AML vs. CLL vs. ALL), plan transplant conditioning, and guide CAR-T cell engineering. Single-cell RNA sequencing has refined the hierarchy from a branching tree to a continuous landscape.

entries.label: Osteoclast–Osteoblast Coupling (RANK/RANKL/OPG Axis)
entries.hint: Bone remodelling is orchestrated through a molecular dialogue: osteoblasts express RANKL (receptor activator of NF-κB ligand), which binds RANK on osteoclast precursors, driving osteoclast differentiation and bone resorption. Osteoblasts also secrete OPG (osteoprotegerin), a decoy receptor that inhibits RANKL — the OPG/RANKL ratio determines the balance of formation and resorption. Targeted RANKL inhibition (denosumab) reduces fracture risk; unbalanced osteoclast activity underlies bone metastasis and post-menopausal osteoporosis.
entries.note: Simonet, W. S. et al. (1997) Cell — OPG discovery. Lacey, D. L. et al. (1998) Cell — RANKL identified as OPG ligand. Yasuda, H. et al. (1998) PNAS — RANK/RANKL receptor-ligand pair. Clinical translation: denosumab (RANK-Fc fusion) approved 2010 for osteoporosis and bone metastasis prevention. The RANK/RANKL/OPG axis also regulates lymph node development, body temperature, and mammary gland development.

categories.label: Core Concepts
categories.subtitle: Fundamental anatomical and histological constructs and structural frameworks

entries.label: The Four Tissue Types
entries.hint: All organs are combinations of four fundamental tissue classes. Epithelium: covering and lining membranes and glands; classified by layer count (simple/stratified) and cell shape (squamous/cuboidal/columnar/transitional/pseudostratified). Connective tissue: support, protection, and transport; includes loose, dense, cartilage, bone, blood, and adipose. Muscle: skeletal (voluntary, striated), cardiac (involuntary, striated, intercalated discs), smooth (involuntary, non-striated). Nervous tissue: excitability and conduction via neurons and supporting glia.
entries.note: Bichat, X. (1800). Traité des membranes — described 21 membrane types from macroscopic dissection without a microscope, anticipating tissue histology. Bichat, X. (1801). Anatomie générale — 'tissue' as the level of organisation between organ and chemistry. Schwann's cell theory (1839) reduced Bichat's 21 types to four families. Bichat died aged 30 (tuberculosis); his bust is in the Paris Panthéon.

entries.label: Anatomical Planes, Regions & Directional Terminology
entries.hint: Anatomical position (standing erect, arms supinated, palms forward, feet flat) is the universal reference frame. Three orthogonal planes: sagittal (divides left/right), coronal/frontal (divides anterior/posterior), transverse/horizontal (divides superior/inferior). Directional pairs: superior/inferior, anterior/posterior, medial/lateral, proximal/distal (limbs), superficial/deep, ipsilateral/contralateral. Nine abdominal regions (or four quadrants) provide clinical localisation shorthand for organ position.
entries.note: Basle Nomina Anatomica (1895) — standardised directional terminology. Nine abdominal regions: right/left hypochondriac, right/left lumbar, right/left iliac (inguinal), epigastric, umbilical, pubic (hypogastric). Clinical usage: McBurney's point (5cm from ASIS on ASIS-umbilicus line) for appendix; Murphy's sign in right hypochondrium for gallbladder. Four-quadrant division: RUQ, LUQ, RLQ, LLQ — used in surgical and emergency contexts.

entries.label: Bone Structure — Compact, Cancellous & the Osteon
entries.hint: Cortical (compact) bone consists of osteons (Haversian systems): 4–20 concentric lamellae around a central Haversian canal containing blood vessels and nerves; interconnected by Volkmann's canals. Osteocytes occupy lacunae within lamellae and communicate via canaliculi (mechanosensing network). Cancellous (trabecular) bone fills metaphyses and flat bones: a lattice of plates and rods whose orientation follows principal stress trajectories. Bone is ~70% mineral (hydroxyapatite), ~30% organic matrix (type I collagen, osteocalcin, osteopontin).
entries.note: Havers, C. (1691). Osteologia Nova — first description of the Haversian canal system. 206 bones in the adult human skeleton; growth plates (epiphyseal cartilage) fuse at 16–25 years. Bone cell types: osteoblasts (formation), osteocytes (maintenance, mechanosensing), osteoclasts (resorption, multinucleated, RANK-expressing). Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) measures bone mineral density; T-score ≤−2.5 defines osteoporosis.

entries.label: Joint Types & Synovial Architecture
entries.hint: Joints are classified by movement (synarthrosis = immovable, amphiarthrosis = slightly movable, diarthrosis = freely movable) and connective tissue type (fibrous/cartilaginous/synovial). Synovial joints have: a fibrous capsule lined by the synovial membrane (type A macrophage-like; type B fibroblast-like synoviocytes secreting hyaluronic acid and lubricin), articular hyaline cartilage (collagen II, aggrecan; avascular, no perichondrium), synovial fluid (plasma dialysate + lubricin + HA). Cyclical loading is required for cartilage nutrition by diffusion.
entries.note: Hunter, W. (1743). 'Of the structure and diseases of articulating cartilages.' Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society — first systematic description of cartilage nutrition and joint disease. Six synovial joint subtypes: ball-and-socket (hip, shoulder), hinge (elbow, knee), pivot (atlantoaxial), condyloid (wrist), saddle (1st carpometacarpal), plane/gliding (acromioclavicular). The knee is the largest synovial joint; the stapes joint in the ear is the smallest.

entries.label: Sarcomere & Sliding Filament Mechanism
entries.loc: MRC Laboratory of Molecular Biology, Cambridge, England
entries.hint: The sarcomere (Z-disk to Z-disk, ~2.2µm at rest) is the contractile unit of skeletal muscle. Thick filaments (myosin II) overlap with thin filaments (actin + tropomyosin + troponin). During contraction, myosin heads undergo ATP-dependent power strokes, pulling actin toward the M-line; filaments slide past each other — neither shortens. The H-zone and I-band narrow; the A-band length stays constant. This predicts that force and shortening velocity are inversely related (force–velocity relationship).
entries.note: Huxley, A. F. & Niedergerke, R. (1954). 'Structural changes in muscle during contraction.' Nature, 173, 971–973. Huxley, H. E. & Hanson, J. (1954). 'Changes in the cross-striations of muscle during contraction.' Nature, 173, 973–976 — two simultaneous papers establishing sliding filament theory. Cross-bridge cycle characterised by Lymn & Taylor (1971) Journal of Biochemistry. Calcium release from SR via ryanodine receptor 1 (RyR1) triggers tropomyosin shift, unblocking actin binding sites.

entries.label: Heart Chambers, Valves & Coronary Circulation
entries.hint: The heart has four chambers in series: right atrium (systemic venous return via SVC/IVC) → tricuspid valve → right ventricle → pulmonary valve → pulmonary arteries → lungs → pulmonary veins → left atrium → mitral valve → left ventricle → aortic valve → aorta. Coronary arteries arise from the aortic sinuses: left main bifurcates into LAD (anterior LV, septum) and LCx (lateral LV); RCA supplies the right heart and (in 70%) the posterior descending artery. The SA node sets heart rate; the AV node delays conduction to allow ventricular filling.
entries.note: Lower, R. (1669). Tractatus de Corde — first systematic cardiac anatomy including coronary vessels. Stensen, N. (1669) independently described the cardiac muscle. The fibrous cardiac skeleton separates atria from ventricles and anchors the four valve annuli. Left ventricular wall thickness ~10–11mm; right ventricular ~3–4mm — reflecting the pressure differential (systemic ~120mmHg vs. pulmonary ~25mmHg). Ejection fraction (EF) >55% is normal; EF <40% defines heart failure.

entries.label: Peripheral Nervous System — Spinal Nerves & Plexuses
entries.hint: 31 pairs of spinal nerves exit via intervertebral foramina: 8 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 sacral, 1 coccygeal. Each has a dorsal root (sensory, with dorsal root ganglion) and ventral root (motor) — the Bell-Magendie Law (1811). Cervical ventral rami (C5–T1) form the brachial plexus (roots → trunks → divisions → cords → branches: musculocutaneous, median, ulnar, radial, axillary). L2–S3 form the lumbosacral plexus (femoral, obturator, sciatic, pudendal). 12 cranial nerves supply the head and neck; CN X (vagus) reaches the thorax and abdomen.
entries.note: Bell, C. (1811). Idea of a New Anatomy of the Brain — ventral roots are motor. Magendie, F. (1822) Journal de Physiologie — sensory/motor root distinction confirmed experimentally. Brachial plexus mnemonic: 'Randy Travis Drinks Cold Beer' (Roots, Trunks, Divisions, Cords, Branches). Saturday Night Palsy: radial nerve compression at spiral groove → wrist drop. Carpal tunnel syndrome: median nerve entrapment → thenar wasting + lateral 3½ digit numbness.

entries.label: Lymphatic Anatomy & Lymph Node Architecture
entries.hint: The lymphatic system returns ~2–4L of interstitial fluid daily to the bloodstream: left-sided and lower-body lymph drains via the thoracic duct to the left subclavian vein; right head/arm via the right lymphatic duct to the right subclavian. Lymph nodes are bean-shaped organs containing a cortex (B-cell follicles with germinal centres), paracortex (T-cell zone), and medullary cords (plasma cells). Afferent lymphatics bring antigen; efferent lymphatics carry the response. Lymphadenopathy localises infection or metastasis to a drainage territory.
entries.note: Rudbeck, O. (1651) and Bartholin, T. (1652) independently described the lymphatic system and thoracic duct. Cisterna chyli (L1–L2): collects lymph from lower limbs and intestinal lacteals (fat absorption). Sentinel lymph node biopsy (Morton, 1992): the first draining node from a tumour predicts regional nodal spread — if negative, dissection is avoided. Virchow's node: left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy from abdominal malignancy (Troisier's sign).

entries.label: Skin Layers & Epidermal Renewal
entries.hint: Skin has three principal layers. Epidermis (stratified squamous keratinised epithelium): basal layer (stratum basale, mitotically active, anchored to basement membrane) → spinous (stratum spinosum, desmosomes, keratin tonofilaments) → granular (lamellar bodies, filaggrin, cornified envelope) → corneum (dead corneocytes, lipid-rich, the true barrier). Dermis: dense irregular connective tissue with collagen I/III, elastin, fibroblasts, hair follicles, sweat glands, Meissner and Pacinian corpuscles. Hypodermis: loose connective tissue and adipocytes. Full epidermal turnover: ~28 days.
entries.note: Purkinjeˇ, J. E. (1833) and Remak, R. (1838) — cell layers of the skin. Skin is the body's largest organ (~1.8m², ~4kg). Melanocytes (1:10 ratio to basal keratinocytes) in stratum basale produce melanin transferred to keratinocytes via melanosomes. Langerhans cells (bone marrow-derived APCs) reside in stratum spinosum. Merkel cells at dermal-epidermal junction are mechanoreceptors (slowly adapting type 1). Psoriasis: epidermal turnover accelerated to ~7 days by IL-17/IL-23 signalling.

entries.label: Extracellular Matrix — Collagen, Elastin & Proteoglycans
entries.hint: The ECM is a non-cellular meshwork of fibrous proteins and ground substance that provides structural support, tissue architecture signals, and a force-transmission medium. Collagen (~30% of total body protein): type I (bone, tendon, skin), type II (cartilage), type III (early wound healing, gut), type IV (basement membranes). Elastin (aorta, lung, skin): allows recoil after stretch (cross-linked tropoelastin). Proteoglycans (aggrecan, perlecan, versican): protein cores with long glycosaminoglycan chains that bind water and provide compressive resistance.
entries.note: Hay, E. D. (ed.) (1981). Cell Biology of Extracellular Matrix. Plenum Press — definitive synthesis. 28 collagen types identified to date; type I is the most abundant protein in the body. Marfan syndrome: fibrillin-1 mutation → aortic dissection and lens dislocation (ECM scaffold failure). Osteogenesis imperfecta: collagen I mutation → brittle bones. Ehlers-Danlos syndrome: multiple collagen/enzyme mutations → hyperelastic skin and hypermobile joints. MMP (matrix metalloproteinases) remodel ECM during development, wound healing, and tumour invasion.

entries.label: GI Histology & Gut Wall Architecture
entries.loc: University of Frankfurt an der Oder, Frankfurt an der Oder, Germany
entries.hint: The GI tract wall shares a universal four-layer architecture from oesophagus to rectum. (1) Mucosa: an inner epithelial lining adapted to local function, with a lamina propria and a muscularis mucosae. (2) Submucosa: dense connective tissue housing Meissner's (submucosal) nerve plexus and Brunner's glands in the duodenum. (3) Muscularis externa: inner circular and outer longitudinal smooth muscle with Auerbach's (myenteric) plexus between them. (4) Serosa (peritoneum-covered) or adventitia (retroperitoneal). The small intestine multiplies absorptive surface area ~600-fold over a flat tube via: plicae circulares (folds), villi (finger projections with a central lacteal and capillary network), and microvilli (brush border of enterocytes).
entries.note: Lieberkühn, J. N. (1745). Dissertatio de Fabrica et Actione Villorum Intestinorum Tenuium — crypts of Lieberkühn (intestinal glands) and villi described. Brunner, J. C. (1688) — Brunner's glands in duodenum. Intestinal cell types: absorptive enterocytes (~80%), goblet cells (mucus secretion), Paneth cells (crypts base, antimicrobial peptides: defensins, lysozyme), enteroendocrine cells (serotonin, GIP, GLP-1), stem cells (Lgr5+ at crypt base: Sato, T. et al. 2009 Nature). The enteric nervous system (ENS) contains ~500 million neurons in Meissner's and Auerbach's plexuses — more than the spinal cord — controlling peristalsis, secretion, and local blood flow independently of the CNS.

entries.label: Pulmonary Alveoli & the Blood-Air Barrier
entries.loc: Rockefeller Institute, New York, New York
entries.hint: The functional respiratory unit is the acinus: terminal bronchiole → respiratory bronchioles → alveolar ducts → alveolar sacs → alveoli (~300 million per lung; total gas-exchange surface area ~70m²). The blood-air barrier is <0.5µm thick, consisting of three fused layers: (1) Type I pneumocytes (squamous, covering 95% of alveolar surface, gas exchange); (2) a fused basement membrane shared between epithelium and endothelium; (3) fenestrated capillary endothelial cells. Type II pneumocytes (granular, 5% of surface area but 60% of alveolar cell count) produce surfactant (primarily dipalmitoyl phosphatidylcholine) that reduces surface tension, preventing alveolar collapse on expiration, and serve as progenitor cells for type I regeneration.
entries.note: Malpighi (1661) first described alveolar structure under early microscopy. Type I/II pneumocyte distinction: Low, F. N. (1952–1953) using electron microscopy. Surfactant discovery: Pattle, R. E. (1955) Nature; Avery, M. E. & Mead, J. (1959) American Journal of Diseases of Children — surfactant deficiency causes infant respiratory distress syndrome (IRDS/hyaline membrane disease). Exogenous surfactant therapy (beractant, poractant alfa) is a cornerstone of neonatal intensive care. In adult ARDS, diffuse alveolar damage destroys type II cells and depletes surfactant, causing widespread atelectasis and refractory hypoxaemia. The Clara (club) cells of bronchioles are the secretory equivalent of type II cells in the conducting zone.

entries.label: Nephron & the Renal Filtration Barrier
entries.hint: Each kidney contains ~1 million nephrons. The nephron: renal corpuscle (glomerulus + Bowman's capsule) → proximal convoluted tubule (PCT: ~65% Na+/water, all glucose/amino acids reabsorbed) → loop of Henle (countercurrent multiplication creating medullary osmotic gradient) → distal convoluted tubule (fine Na+/Ca2+ regulation under aldosterone/PTH) → collecting duct (ADH-regulated water reabsorption; acid-base). The glomerular filtration barrier has three layers acting as a size- and charge-selective sieve: (1) fenestrated capillary endothelium with negatively charged glycocalyx repelling albumin; (2) glomerular basement membrane (type IV collagen, laminin, heparan sulphate, 250–350nm thick); (3) visceral epithelial cells (podocytes) with interdigitating foot processes bridged by slit diaphragms (nephrin, podocin proteins).
entries.note: Bowman, W. (1842). 'On the structure and use of the Malpighian bodies of the kidney.' Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society, 132, 57–80 — described the glomerulus, Bowman's capsule, and proposed filtration. Malpighi (1666) first described the glomerulus as a 'Malpighian body.' Nephrin gene (NPHS1): Kestilä, M. et al. (1998) Molecular Cell — mutations cause congenital nephrotic syndrome (Finnish type). Podocin (NPHS2): Boute, N. et al. (2000) Nature Genetics. Clinical: glomerulonephritis damages the filtration barrier; dipstick proteinuria + haematuria localises pathology to the glomerulus. GFR estimated by CKD-EPI formula. In diabetic nephropathy, GBM thickening and podocyte loss precede clinical proteinuria by years.

categories.label: Methods & Techniques
categories.subtitle: Technologies and techniques for visualising and investigating anatomical structure

entries.label: Cadaveric Dissection
entries.hint: Systematic dissection of human cadavers — dorsal to ventral, superficial to deep, regional — remains the primary method of gross anatomy education. Vesalius standardised the approach in 1543. Modern anatomy dissection uses formalin-fixed cadavers (paraformaldehyde crosslinks proteins, preventing decomposition) or plastinated specimens (von Hagens, 1977). Dissection develops three-dimensional spatial reasoning that imaging-based instruction only partially replicates: appreciation of fascial planes, anatomical variation, and tissue consistency.
entries.note: Vesalius (1543) — performed dissections himself rather than delegating to barber-surgeons. Formalin fixation: Blum, F. (1893) Zeitschrift für wissenschaftliche Mikroskopie — formaldehyde as preservative. Plastination: Hagens, G. von (1979) Anatomia, Histologia, Embryologia — polymer replacement of tissue water. The Anatomy Act (UK, 1832) and the National Organ Transplant Act (US, 1984) regulate body donation; many countries require written informed consent.

entries.label: Histological Staining — H&E & Special Stains
entries.hint: Haematoxylin (basic dye) stains acidic structures blue-purple: nuclei, ribosomes, rough ER. Eosin (acidic dye) stains basic/eosinophilic structures pink: cytoplasm, collagen, muscle fibres. H&E is the universal screening stain in histopathology — it distinguishes cell type, architecture, mitotic activity, and most pathological changes. Special stains target specific structures: PAS (glycogen and fungi, magenta), Masson's trichrome (collagen blue/green), Prussian blue (iron, blue), Ziehl-Neelsen (acid-fast mycobacteria, red), Congo red (amyloid; green birefringence under polarised light).
entries.note: Böhmer, F. (1865) — first haematoxylin stain. H&E combination standardised: ~1876. Ehrlich, P. (1879) — eosin as a cytoplasmic counterstain. Gram stain (1884): crystal violet + iodine mordant → differentiate gram-positive (purple) from gram-negative (pink) bacteria in tissue. Congo red + polarised light for amyloid: Divry, P. & Florkin, M. (1927). WHO tumour classification (5th ed., 2022) is built entirely on H&E morphology plus IHC and molecular markers.

entries.label: Radiographic Imaging — X-ray & Fluoroscopy
entries.hint: Plain radiographs use differential X-ray attenuation to image anatomy by density. Five radiodensities: gas (black), fat (dark grey), water/soft tissue (grey), bone/calcium (white), metal (bright white). The Ottawa Rules (1992) — validated clinical decision rules — safely reduce ankle and knee X-rays by 35% without missing fractures. Fluoroscopy provides real-time X-ray: essential for cardiac catheterisation, GI contrast studies, and intraoperative orthopaedic guidance. DR (digital radiography) has replaced film; PACS archives and distributes images electronically.
entries.note: Röntgen, W. C. (1895) — discovery; Nobel Prize 1901. Ottawa Ankle Rules: Stiell, I. G. et al. (1992) JAMA — point tenderness at malleoli or inability to weight-bear indicates X-ray need. Effective radiation dose: chest X-ray ~0.02mSv; pelvis X-ray ~0.7mSv; chest CT ~7mSv (cf. background ~3mSv/year). Lead shielding protects gonads and thyroid from scatter. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA): iodinated contrast with fluoroscopy images vascular anatomy.

entries.label: Electron Microscopy — TEM & SEM
entries.loc: Technical University of Berlin, Berlin, Germany
entries.hint: Transmission EM (TEM) fires electrons through ultrathin sections (50–100nm) stained with heavy metals (osmium tetroxide, uranyl acetate) for contrast. Resolution ~0.2nm — 1000× better than light microscopy. Revealed sarcomere ultrastructure, synaptic vesicles, nuclear pore complex, tight junctions, and basement membrane laminae. Scanning EM (SEM) scans gold-coated surfaces, producing 3D topographic images. Cryo-EM vitrifies hydrated specimens and resolves protein structures at near-atomic resolution (Nobel Chemistry 2017: Dubochet, Frank, Henderson).
entries.note: Ruska, E. & Knoll, M. (1932) — first TEM prototype; Nobel Physics 1986 (Ruska). Sarcomere structure: Huxley, H. E. (1953) Acta Crystallographica. Synaptic vesicles: De Robertis, E. D. P. & Bennett, H. S. (1955) Journal of Biophysical and Biochemical Cytology — first EM of synapse. Cryo-EM revolution: structures of ribosomes, GPCR complexes, and ion channels resolved at 2–3Å without crystallisation. FIB-SEM and serial block-face SEM enable 3D volume reconstruction of neural circuits (connectomics).

entries.label: Cross-Sectional Imaging — CT & MRI
entries.hint: CT (Hounsfield & Cormack, 1972) reconstructs cross-sectional images from multiple X-ray projections; Hounsfield units (HU) quantify tissue density: air (−1000), water (0), bone (+700 to +1000). Multi-detector CT enables isovolumetric reconstruction in any plane and CT angiography with contrast bolus timing. MRI (Lauterbur & Mansfield, 1973) uses proton resonance: T1-weighted images show anatomy (fat bright, fluid dark); T2-weighted images highlight pathology (fluid bright, hence 'water is white on T2'). No ionising radiation in MRI.
entries.note: Hounsfield, G. N. & Cormack, A. M. — Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 1979. First clinical CT: atkinson Morley Hospital, 1972 — brain tumour in a 41-year-old woman. Lauterbur, P. C. & Mansfield, P. — Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2003. MRI contraindications: pacemakers, ferromagnetic implants, cochlear implants (most modern are MRI-conditional). PET-CT combines functional metabolic imaging (18F-FDG) with CT anatomy for cancer staging.

entries.label: Immunohistochemistry (IHC)
entries.hint: A primary antibody specific to the target antigen is applied to a tissue section; a secondary antibody conjugated to a chromogen (DAB, brown; AEC, red) or fluorophore detects the primary. IHC enables identification of cell type (cytokeratins → carcinoma; CD20 → B-cell lymphoma; CD3 → T-cell), proliferation (Ki-67), hormone receptors (ER/PR in breast cancer), and infectious agents (CMV inclusion bodies) in routine pathological tissue. Antigen retrieval (heat-induced epitope retrieval, HIER) is required after formalin fixation to unmask cross-linked epitopes.
entries.note: Coons, A. H. et al. (1941). 'Immunological properties of an antibody containing a fluorescent group.' Proceedings of the Society for Experimental Biology and Medicine, 47, 200–202 — first fluorescent antibody labelling. Enzyme-linked IHC (DAB chromogen): Nakane, P. K. & Pierce, G. B. (1966). Multiplex IHC (mIHC): simultaneous detection of 5–8 markers on a single section using tyramide signal amplification. Digital pathology with IHC: FDA-cleared AI-assisted PD-L1 scoring, Ki-67 quantification.

entries.label: Plastination (von Hagens, 1977)
entries.hint: Tissue water and lipids are replaced with curable polymers under vacuum: silicone rubber for opaque slices, epoxy resin for transparent sections, polyester for brain. The result is a dry, odourless, flexible specimen retaining colour, texture and three-dimensional architecture indefinitely without refrigeration or chemical storage. Plastinated specimens can be handled repeatedly — transforming anatomy education beyond the dissection room and enabling the BODY WORLDS public exhibitions seen by >50 million people worldwide.
entries.note: Hagens, G. von (1979). 'Impregnation of soft biological specimens with thermosetting resins and elastomers.' Anatomia, Histologia, Embryologia, 8, 263–270. BODY WORLDS exhibitions: first public display 1995, Mannheim; now permanent in numerous cities. Forensic application: plastinated cross-sections in forensic pathology for medicolegal teaching. Controversy: ethics of informed consent for public display of plastinated cadavers; legal cases in Germany (1997), Australia (2009).

categories.label: Key Figures
categories.subtitle: Anatomists, histologists and physicians who established the discipline

entries.label: Andreas Vesalius (1514–1564)
entries.hint: Belgian anatomist who performed human dissections himself — radical in an era when professors read Galen aloud while barber-surgeons dissected below. De Humani Corporis Fabrica (1543) replaced textual authority with direct empirical observation, transforming anatomy into an evidence-based discipline. Appointed imperial physician to Charles V and Philip II of Spain. Died on the return from a pilgrimage to Jerusalem, aged 49.
entries.note: Born André Wytinck van Wesel in Brussels. Studied in Louvain, Paris, and Padua. Published the Fabrica and Epitome simultaneously. His public dissections at Padua drew large audiences. The Padua anatomy theatre (built 1594) remains the world's oldest surviving anatomy theatre. Modern Vesalius scholarship: Vesalius, A. (2014). The Fabric of the Human Body — annotated English translation (Northwestern).

entries.label: William Harvey (1578–1657)
entries.hint: English physician who quantitatively proved blood circulates rather than ebbs and flows. Physician Extraordinary to James I and Charles I — his royalist sympathies damaged his later career. Besides circulation, published an embryology text (1651) arguing all animals arise from eggs: 'ex ovo omnia.' The quantitative logic of his argument was the first mathematical proof in physiology.
entries.note: Harvey studied under Fabricius ab Aquapendente in Padua, who had described venous valves (1603) without understanding their implication. Harvey presented his circulation theory to the College of Physicians in London in 1616 — 12 years before publication. Elected Lumleian Lecturer (anatomy) at the College. His library was destroyed in the Great Fire of London (1666). The Harvey circulation claim went from heresy to consensus within his lifetime.

entries.label: Giovanni Battista Morgagni (1682–1771)
entries.hint: Italian anatomist considered the father of anatomical pathology. De Sedibus et Causis Morborum per Anatomen Indagatis (1761) — published at age 79 — correlated clinical histories of 700 patients with post-mortem findings, establishing that disease has anatomical seats and symptoms map to organ pathology. Founded the idea of clinico-pathological correlation, which remains the core structure of pathology.
entries.note: Morgagni held the chair of anatomy at Padua for 56 years (1715–1771). De Sedibus: five books covering head, thorax, abdomen, surgical disorders, and universal diseases — 700 cases, each with clinical history and post-mortem correlation. Multiple eponyms: Morgagni's hernia (diaphragmatic), Morgagni's columns (anal), Morgagni's hydatid (appendix testis). Died at age 89 — one of the oldest major figures in medical history.

entries.label: Xavier Bichat (1771–1802)
entries.hint: French anatomist who described 21 tissue types from macroscopic dissection — without a microscope — anticipating histology. Traité des membranes (1800) and Anatomie générale (1801) founded general anatomy as a discipline: the body is organised at the tissue level, between the organ and the chemical. Performed ~600 post-mortems in a single year. Died of tuberculosis aged 30; his bust is in the Panthéon, Paris.
entries.note: Bichat's Traité identified tissues as having characteristic properties (extensibility, sensibility, contractility) regardless of which organ they appeared in — a conceptual step beyond organ-based anatomy. Napoleon called him 'the most difficult to replace among those who have been lost to France.' The Hôpital Lariboisière and Hôpital Bichat in Paris bear his name. His work preceded cell theory by 38 years, making it conceptually independent of cellular biology.

entries.label: Rudolf Virchow (1821–1902)
entries.hint: German pathologist who completed cell theory with 'omnis cellula e cellula' and founded cellular pathology. Named leukaemia, thrombosis, embolism, and amyloid degeneration in the same decade. Also a liberal politician (Reichstag), public health reformer who engineered Berlin's sewer system, and founder of physical anthropology. Bismarck once challenged him to a duel — Virchow reportedly chose pork sausages as his weapons.
entries.note: Virchow's political activities cost him his Berlin professorship in 1849 (after agitating for reform following the 1848 Revolution); he was reinstated in 1856. The Virchow triad (stasis, hypercoagulability, endothelial injury) remains the framework for understanding venous thromboembolism. He opposed Lister's antiseptic theory and Semmelweis's handwashing argument — errors that tempered his extraordinary legacy.

entries.label: Santiago Ramón y Cajal (1852–1934)
entries.hint: Spanish histologist who established the neuron doctrine through meticulous Golgi silver-stain preparations of spinal cord, cerebellum, and retina. Nobel Prize 1906 (shared with Golgi). An amateur artist before medicine; his neuroanatomical drawings remain scientifically accurate today. Described the axon growth cone, dendritic spines, and directionality of nerve impulse. Also published foundational work on CNS degeneration and the limits of neural regeneration.
entries.note: Cajal was also a photographer, body-builder, and chess player. He learned Golgi's technique from a demonstration slide in 1887 and within 2 years had produced hundreds of landmark preparations. His archive of slides and drawings is held in the Cajal Institute, Madrid. Degeneration/regeneration: Cajal, S. R. y (1928) Degeneration and Regeneration of the Nervous System — still cited. He correctly predicted that CNS axons cannot regenerate because of environmental inhibition, not intrinsic incapacity.

entries.label: Henry Gray (1827–1861)
entries.hint: English anatomist who produced the definitive anatomical text — Gray's Anatomy (1858) — with Henry Vandyke Carter as illustrator. A Fellow of the Royal Society at 25 (for work on the development of the optic vesicle and retina). Gray died of confluent smallpox in 1861, aged ~34, having contracted it from a nephew he was nursing. His book outlived him by 165+ years and remains in continuous print.
entries.note: Gray was demonstrator of anatomy at St George's Hospital, London. The 1858 first edition had 750 pages and 363 illustrations. The book was dedicated to Sir Benjamin Collins Brodie, his surgical mentor. The current edition (42nd, 2020) has over 1,600 pages. Gray's early death — and Carter's long obscurity — make theirs one of the most poignant stories in medical publishing history.

entries.label: Walther Flemming (1843–1905)
entries.hint: German anatomist and cell biologist who pioneered aniline-dye staining of dividing salamander cells, producing the first clear visual record of chromosomes in motion. His meticulous illustrations made the mechanics of cell division observable for the first time, directly enabling the chromosome theory of heredity that followed in the next generation. His two-volume Zellsubstanz, Kern und Zelltheilung (1882) set the visual and descriptive standard for all subsequent cytology.
entries.note: Flemming coined the terms 'chromatin' (1879) and 'mitosis' (1882). He described the equatorial plate (metaphase plate), chromosome splitting, and polar movement — all confirmed by later electron microscopy. He did not realise chromosomes were constant in number per species — this was established by van Beneden (1883) and Boveri (1888). Flemming did not connect chromatin to heredity; this synthesis came from Boveri, Sutton, and ultimately Morgan.

entries.label: Marcello Malpighi (1628–1694)
entries.hint: Italian microscopist who discovered capillaries, the renal glomerulus, splenic white pulp, taste buds on the tongue, and the deep layers of the epidermis (stratum Malpighii). Physician to Pope Innocent XII (1691). Used early microscopy to transform gross anatomy into microscopic anatomy — bridging the macroscopic structure of Harvey's circulation and the invisible cellular world. Elected Fellow of the Royal Society (1668).
entries.note: Malpighi's capillary discovery (1661) completed Harvey's circulatory circuit 33 years after De Motu Cordis. His De Formatione Pulli in Ovo (1672) — on chick embryo development — was the first detailed account of embryogenesis. He also described insect anatomy (silkworm) and plant anatomy (De Anatomia Plantarum). Multiple eponyms: Malpighian layer (epidermis), Malpighian body (renal corpuscle), Malpighian body (splenic white pulp), Malpighian tubule (insect excretory organ).

entries.label: Henry Vandyke Carter (1831–1897)
entries.hint: English artist-physician who created 363 original woodcut illustrations for Gray's Anatomy (1858) — the visual grammar of anatomy for 165+ years. Received no royalties and no co-authorship on the title page; acknowledged only in the introduction. Spent his career as an Indian Medical Service officer in Bombay, where he contributed landmark research on leprosy and mycetoma (Madura foot). His precise, instructional illustration style set the standard for medical art.
entries.note: Carter was a skilled artist before studying medicine; he drew specimens at St George's Hospital under Gray's direction, making preparations and drawing them the same day. His later career in India was distinguished: he was one of the first to distinguish leprosy's two forms (tuberculoid vs. lepromatous) and to identify the fungal cause of mycetoma. He was promoted to Deputy Surgeon-General. His contribution to Gray's was formally acknowledged only after his death. A biography: Richardson, R. (2008) The Making of Mr Gray's Anatomy. Oxford University Press.

entries.label: Galen of Pergamon (129–216 AD)
entries.hint: Roman physician (Greek origin) whose anatomical system — derived from dissections of Barbary apes, pigs, and gladiators' wounds, not intact human cadavers — dominated Western medicine for 1,400 years. Described four humours, two blood systems (venous from liver, arterial from heart), and pores in the interventricular septum. Vesalius's systematic refutation in 1543 ended this dominance. Galen's errors endured because human dissection was prohibited by Roman law and social taboo.
entries.note: Galen served as physician to gladiators in Pergamon (157–162 AD) — giving him experience of wounds — and later as physician to Marcus Aurelius. He wrote >500 treatises; ~300 survive. His four humours (blood/phlegm/yellow bile/black bile) mapped onto Hippocratic qualities (hot/cold/wet/dry). Galen's teleological view — every anatomical structure exists for a purpose — was compatible with Christian theology, which partly explains the long dominance of Galenism. Modern revaluation: Nutton, V. (2004) Ancient Medicine. Routledge.

entries.label: Frank Netter (1906–1991)
entries.hint: American physician-artist who produced the Atlas of Human Anatomy (1989) and the 8-volume CIBA Collection of Medical Illustrations — the most widely used anatomical illustrations in 20th-century clinical practice. Trained as a surgeon but abandoned clinical medicine for art during the Depression. His combination of artistic precision, clinical context, and clear colour coding transformed medical communication and set the standard for all subsequent medical illustration.
entries.note: Netter trained at NYU College of Medicine and interned at Bellevue Hospital. His first commercial medical illustration was for Novartis (then CIBA) in 1936; he produced illustrations for them exclusively for decades. The CIBA Collection of Medical Illustrations (8 volumes, 1948–1993) covers the complete human body. Netter's Atlas of Human Anatomy (1989, first edition) is now in its 8th edition (2022) and remains the standard supplementary atlas in medical education worldwide.

categories.label: Debates & Open Questions
categories.subtitle: Controversies, contested claims and unresolved questions in anatomy and histology

entries.label: Galenic Circulation vs. Harvey's Closed Circuit
entries.hint: For 1,400 years, Galen's model held that the liver produces venous blood, consumed by tissues; the heart generates pneuma in arterial blood; and the interventricular septum has pores allowing venous blood to seep into the arterial system. Harvey's De Motu Cordis (1628) contradicted each claim quantitatively, but faced fierce opposition from Galenic physicians throughout the 1630s–50s. The debate illustrates how quantitative anatomical argument can be resisted when it conflicts with theological and institutional authority.
entries.note: Principal opponents of Harvey: James Primrose (1630) Exercitationes; Jean Riolan the Younger (1649) — argued that only a small portion of blood circulates. Harvey's final rebuttal: De Circulatione Sanguinis (1649). Resolution came not through a single experiment but through accumulation of confirmations: Malpighi's capillaries (1661), Lower's heart-lung experiments (1669), Haller's quantitative physiology (1757). The debate is a case study in the sociology of scientific revolution.

entries.label: Neuron Doctrine vs. Reticular Theory
entries.hint: Cajal (neuron doctrine) and Golgi (reticular theory) shared the 1906 Nobel Prize while fundamentally disagreeing about nervous system structure. Golgi maintained the nerve network was a continuous protoplasmic syncytium; Cajal demonstrated discrete cellular units separated by junctions. Golgi used his Nobel lecture to attack the neuron doctrine. Electron microscopy (De Robertis & Bennett; Palade & Palay, 1954–1955) definitively showed synaptic clefts between cells — resolving the debate in Cajal's favour.
entries.note: A rare case of two Nobel laureates publicly disagreeing in the same ceremony. Golgi's reticulum theory was not entirely wrong — it anticipated the concept of gap junctions (electrical synapses), where cells are electrically coupled. The neuron doctrine debate also presaged the later debate between the 'labelled line' and 'across-fibre pattern' theories of sensory coding. Shepherd, G. M. (2016). Foundations of the Neuron Doctrine. Oxford University Press — history of the debate.

entries.label: Cardiac Muscle Regeneration — Can the Heart Repair Itself?
entries.loc: New York Medical College, New York, New York
entries.hint: Beltrami et al. (2001) Science reported resident cardiac stem cells (c-Kit+) capable of generating new cardiomyocytes after myocardial infarction — challenging the dogma that the adult heart cannot regenerate. This spawned the SCIPIO clinical trial programme. Anversa's lab produced multiple high-profile papers; most were retracted 2018–2021 following evidence of data fabrication. The correct answer: the adult heart renews ~1% of cardiomyocytes/year (Bergmann et al. 2009, C14 dating), insufficient for meaningful repair.
entries.note: Beltrami, A. P. et al. (2001). 'Evidence that human cardiac myocytes divide after myocardial infarction.' New England Journal of Medicine, 344, 1750–1757. Anversa, P. et al. (2003) — c-Kit+ CSCs claim (later retracted). SCIPIO trial: Bolli, R. et al. (2011) Lancet — results could not be reproduced; paper was subsequently corrected and disputed. Bergmann, O. et al. (2009). 'Evidence for cardiomyocyte renewal in humans.' Science, 324, 98–102 — C14 dating from atomic bomb fallout. The controversy caused long-term damage to the cardiac regeneration field.

entries.label: Fascia Classification — What Is Fascia?
entries.hint: The 2007 and 2012 International Fascia Research Congresses exposed profound definitional disagreement: 'fascia' variously refers to discrete fibrous sheets (superficial fascia, deep fascia, epimysium), all fibrous connective tissue in the body, or any tissue that transmits mechanical force. Radiologists, surgeons, physiotherapists, and anatomists use the term differently. The Fascia Nomenclature Committee (2013) attempted standardisation: fascia = 'sheath, sheet, or dissectible aggregation of connective tissue forming beneath the skin to attach, enclose, and separate muscles and internal organs.' Force transmission significance remains unresolved.
entries.note: Schleip, R. et al. (2012). Fascia Research Congress — proceedings. Stecco, C. et al. (2011) Clinical Anatomy — histological definition controversy. Huijing, P. A. & Baan, G. C. (2001) Archives of Physiology and Biochemistry — epimysial force transmission. The debate has practical consequences: surgical planes differ depending on whether 'fascia' is treated as a distinct layer or as part of continuous connective tissue. The concept of the 'fascial system' in manual therapy (Rolfing, osteopathy) has outrun the anatomical evidence.

entries.label: The 'Normal' Anatomy Atlas — Misleading Standard?
entries.hint: Standard anatomy atlases depict a young, male, European body in average proportions — a 'Netter man.' Anatomical variation is pervasive: the Circle of Willis is complete in only ~20–25% of individuals; the palmaris longus is absent in ~14%; the number of lumbar vertebrae varies from 4 to 6; the hepatic artery follows the 'textbook' course in only ~55%. Students trained on atlases develop inaccurate expectations of real patients. Diversity of reference bodies and explicit coverage of variation are active areas of anatomy curriculum reform.
entries.note: Hartkamp, M. J. et al. (1999) Stroke — Circle of Willis completeness in 1,149 patients (MRA). Sanudo, J. R. et al. (2003) — palmaris longus absence rates by population. Bergman, R. A. et al. (1988). Compendium of Human Anatomical Variation — catalogued >10,000 documented anatomical variants. Turney, B. W. (2007) Journal of the Royal Society of Medicine — 'what can surgeons learn from anatomists?' The female body is systematically underrepresented in anatomy education, which contributes to diagnostic delays in women presenting with atypical symptoms.

entries.label: Glymphatic System — Does the Brain Have a Lymph Pathway?
entries.loc: University of Rochester, Rochester, New York
entries.hint: Iliff et al. (2013) Science proposed that CSF enters the brain along paravascular spaces driven by arterial pulsations, exchanges with interstitial fluid via AQP4 water channels on astrocytic endfeet, and drains waste (including amyloid-β) along venous paravascular spaces to cervical lymph nodes. Sleep enhances glymphatic clearance ~60%. The system has been contested: some groups cannot reproduce directional flow or AQP4 dependence; the hydraulic driving force is debated. If real, glymphatic dysfunction could explain amyloid accumulation in Alzheimer's disease.
entries.note: Iliff, J. J. et al. (2013). 'A paravascular pathway facilitates CSF flow through the brain parenchyma.' Science Translational Medicine, 5, 111ra119. AQP4 challenge: Hladky, S. B. & Barrand, M. A. (2018) Fluids and Barriers of the CNS — bulk flow via AQP4 energetically implausible. Sleep enhancement: Xie, L. et al. (2013) Science. Cerebral lymphatics: Louveau, A. et al. (2015) Nature — dural lymphatic vessels confirmed in mice and humans. The 'glymphatic' term was coined by Maiken Nedergaard's group (combining 'glial' and 'lymphatic').

entries.label: Dermatome Maps — Which Atlas Is Correct?
entries.hint: Dermatome boundaries in major anatomical atlases (Keegan & Garrett 1948; Foerster 1933; Hansen & Schliack 1962) disagree by up to two spinal segments for many regions. A systematic comparison of 11 dermatome maps by Downs & Laporte (2011) found significant inter-map disagreement in 90% of tested regions. Sources of disagreement: herpes zoster distribution vs. posterior root section vs. nerve block studies; extent of adjacent segment overlap; and individual anatomical variation. Clinical implication: dermatomal maps are guides, not precise boundaries.
entries.note: Downs, M. B. & Laporte, C. (2011). 'Conflicting dermatome maps: educational and clinical implications.' Journal of Orthopaedic & Sports Physical Therapy, 41, 427–434. Keegan, J. J. & Garrett, F. D. (1948) Anatomical Record — mapping from posterior column lesions. Foerster, O. (1933) Brain — posterior root section. The L4 dermatome illustrates the problem: mapped to medial shin (Keegan-Garrett), anterior shin (Foerster), or lateral shin (Head). A consensus map remains elusive.

entries.label: Is the Mesentery a New Organ?
entries.hint: Coffey & O'Leary (2016) Lancet Gastroenterology & Hepatology proposed that the mesentery is a single continuous organ — a fold of peritoneum connecting all intestinal segments to the posterior abdominal wall — rather than a fragmented series of ligaments. The claim was widely reported as the 'discovery of a new organ.' Critics noted that mesenteric continuity was anatomically well known since Treitz (1853) and that the designation 'organ' was definitional reframing, not anatomical discovery.
entries.note: Coffey, J. C. & O'Leary, D. P. (2016). 'The mesentery: structure, function, and role in disease.' Lancet Gastroenterology & Hepatology, 1, 238–247. Treitz, W. (1853) — described the mesentery as a continuous fold. The controversy highlights the lack of consensus on what constitutes an 'organ': is it a structure with a distinct boundary, a distinct function, or a distinct clinical relevance? The mesentery does have surgical relevance — 'complete mesocolic excision' in colon cancer surgery is guided by mesenteric anatomy.

categories.label: Clinical & Educational Applications
categories.subtitle: How anatomical and histological knowledge translates to clinical practice and education

entries.label: Surgical Anatomy & Operative Planning
entries.hint: Pre-operative CT/MRI cross-sectional anatomy defines safe dissection planes, vessel relationships, and anomalies before every major operation. Image-guided surgery (IGS) uses registered imaging to navigate instruments in real time. Laparoscopic anatomy has forced re-conceptualisation of traditional open-surgery anatomy: views are rotated 90°, fascial planes are identified visually rather than by touch. Key anatomical landmarks guide safe dissection: McBurney's point (appendix), Hesselbach's triangle (direct inguinal hernia), Calot's triangle (cholecystectomy — cystic artery and cystic duct).
entries.note: Image-guided neurosurgery: Galloway, R. L. (2001) Annual Review of Biomedical Engineering. Laparoscopic anatomy teaching: Fried, G. M. et al. (1994) American Journal of Surgery — cadaveric laparoscopic training model. Safe cholecystectomy: Strasberg, S. M. (1995) Journal of the American College of Surgeons — 'critical view of safety' technique. Robotic surgery (da Vinci, approved 2000) uses 3D magnified anatomical views; the surgeon must translate anatomical knowledge from flat atlas views to high-resolution endoscopic perspective.

entries.label: Histopathology & Tissue Diagnosis
entries.hint: Tissue diagnosis by H&E microscopy and IHC is the gold standard for cancer diagnosis, staging, and subtyping: tumour type (adenocarcinoma vs. squamous cell), grade (differentiation), margin status, invasion depth, vascular and perineural invasion, receptor status (ER/PR/HER2 in breast cancer), and PD-L1 expression for immunotherapy eligibility. Frozen section intraoperative biopsy takes ~20 minutes and guides the extent of surgical resection in real time. Digital pathology (whole-slide imaging) and AI-assisted scoring are transforming throughput and reproducibility.
entries.note: Virchow (1858) — cellular pathology as foundation of histopathology. Frozen section: Cullen, T. S. (1895) — first intraoperative frozen section for gynaecological tumour. HER2 IHC/ISH: Wolff, A. C. et al. (2007) ASCO/CAP guidelines — critical for trastuzumab eligibility. FDA-cleared AI pathology: Paige Prostate (2021) for prostate cancer detection. Whole-slide imaging platforms: Aperio (Leica), Ventana, Hamamatsu. WHO Classification of Tumours (5th ed.) standardises histological diagnostic criteria globally.

entries.label: Orthopaedic Biomechanics & Joint Replacement
entries.hint: Total hip replacement (Charnley 1962) and total knee replacement (Gunston 1971) are among the most successful surgical operations by functional outcome and quality-adjusted life years gained. Implant design is governed by anatomical geometry (femoral neck-shaft angle, acetabular anteversion 15–20°), biomechanical loading (5–10× body weight at the hip during stair climbing), and material science (UHMWPE, cobalt-chromium, ceramics). Failure modes — aseptic loosening, polyethylene wear, fracture, dislocation, periprosthetic infection — all require anatomical knowledge for prevention and revision.
entries.note: Charnley, J. (1960). 'Arthroplasty of the hip: a new operation.' Lancet, 1, 1129–1132. Charnley's low-friction arthroplasty: 22mm femoral head, UHMWPE socket, acrylic cement fixation. Annual implantations: >400,000 THR and >700,000 TKR in the US (2020). Registry data: National Joint Registry (UK, 2003–present) — 10-year implant survival ~95% for primary THR. Computer-assisted surgery and patient-specific cutting guides (PSI) use CT-derived 3D anatomy models to plan implant positioning.

entries.label: Radiological Anatomy & Medical Imaging
entries.hint: Radiological anatomy — interpreting body structures in CT/MRI/ultrasound/PET — is the dominant applied branch of anatomy in clinical practice. The radiologist maps disease by relating imaging features to anatomical position, tissue density, and signal characteristics. Key applications: CT angiography for aortic dissection (timing the contrast bolus sweep), MRI for soft tissue characterisation (disc herniation at L4/5 compressing L5 nerve root), ultrasound for real-time procedural guidance (central venous line, joint injection, nerve block), PET-CT for metabolically active lesions. Patient-specific 3D-printed anatomy models are emerging for surgical planning.
entries.note: First diagnostic CT: Hounsfield & Ambrose (1973) British Journal of Radiology — first clinical brain scan published. Diagnostic ultrasound: Wild & Reid (1952) — first B-mode ultrasound images of soft tissue. PET imaging: Ter-Pogossian (1975). CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA): Remy-Jardin et al. (1992) Radiology — now gold standard for pulmonary embolism diagnosis. The Royal College of Radiologists (UK) and ACR (US) publish guidance on appropriate use and radiation dose optimisation (ALARA principle).

entries.label: Anatomical Education — Plastination & Virtual Anatomy
entries.hint: Anatomical education is undergoing its most significant reform since Vesalius. Plastinated specimens have made dissection-quality anatomy accessible without cadaveric constraints. Virtual anatomy tables (Anatomage, zSpace) allow full-body virtual dissection at CT-grade resolution. The net direction: from cadaveric memorisation toward clinical imaging and functional anatomy. A key controversy: do students trained without cadaveric dissection develop adequate three-dimensional spatial reasoning and the professional attitudes (respect for the body, acceptance of mortality) that traditional dissection uniquely fosters?
entries.note: von Hagens plastination (1977) — me7. Anatomage Table: introduced 2006 (Stanford University Medical Center); now in >300 medical schools. Augmented reality anatomy: HoloAnatomy (Case Western, 2016) using Microsoft HoloLens. Competency study: McLachlan, J. C. et al. (2004) Medical Education — students taught anatomy without dissection performed equally in clinical assessments. Counter-evidence: Older, J. (2004) ANZ Journal of Surgery — reduced surgical competence correlates with reduced dissection hours.

entries.label: Tissue Engineering & Regenerative Medicine
entries.loc: Wake Forest Institute, Winston-Salem, North Carolina
entries.hint: Tissue engineering constructs living tissues ex vivo by seeding cells onto biomimetic scaffolds (decellularised ECM, hydrogels, 3D-bioprinted structures) that provide structural and biochemical cues. Landmarks: first tissue-engineered bladder implanted in a patient (Atala 2006), organoid cultures reproducing intestinal crypts, liver ducts, and kidney tubules for disease modelling. The field bridges histology, materials science, and surgery. Most organ-scale applications remain preclinical; skin grafts and tracheal patches are approved for clinical use.
entries.note: Atala, A. et al. (2006). 'Tissue-engineered autologous bladders for patients needing cystoplasty.' Lancet, 367, 1241–1246 — first implanted tissue-engineered organ. Intestinal organoids: Sato, T. et al. (2009) Nature — Lgr5+ stem cells form self-organising intestinal crypts in Matrigel. 3D bioprinting: Kang, H.-W. et al. (2016) Nature Biotechnology — ITOP printer fabricates cartilage, bone, and muscle constructs with embedded vasculature. Trachea controversy: Macchiarini (2008–2014) tracheal transplant programme — multiple patient deaths and fraud investigation.
